item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yea, the buuny was because of the image. I found it on an old local site I worked for, an ad for a little bunny shelf described here, with the ad written horribly and it looking so strange. The ad was already over 7 years old, and I was very lucky to get verbal permission from the ad holder to use their image (didnt expect a responce actually), but I couldn't get anything more concrete so the mods removed my image. You're welcome to remove the vote as I don't expect to get back to this, I lost motivation when I lost the image it was all based on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was the reason this is named "buuny" because of the image? If so, now that the image can't be in the SCP, I'd recommend changing the name to Bunny Butler. The two 'u's don't do anything to help the page, and make it feel gimmicky.

As for the SCP itself, I feel like it could be fleshed out more. I think there's a lot of potential. Maybe give it a darker turn, something worse than the machete? That would be my guess.

For now, tentatively Upvoting.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: You may as well just take it down because im on vacation and have no way to access the seller any longer. I'll try to build my own when i get back or something.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

In this case, whilst permission from the original image creator is sufficient, proof must be posted of such permission being given.

EDIT: Image removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To make it all less confusing, I reduced it to just g. Science isn't my strongest area of expertise. Thanks for the help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That in all seriousness is the reason "gm" is not an officially recognized abbreviation for grams: Gm is gigameters, gm is gram meters. The latter pairing pops up occasionally in vector physics- it's a less convoluted way of writing mN⋅s2. It comes up when you're doing things like calculating the total change in momentum in a system over a given time period or similarly odd things. Grams per meter is g/m, and people who refer to that as gram meters are incorrect in doing so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I googled it, and what came up for gm was gigameters or grams. If it was a gram-meter (which a google search turns up as either grams per meters (a linear density measurement) or a unit of energy) then it makes even less sense to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because I like the non-sequitur weirdness here, but definitely could be improved. In particular, seconding that little more troll logic and weird-sales-pitch interaction from the "buuny" would go a long way for characterization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The abbreviation for gram is g; "gm" would be gram-meters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image doesn't look like it's 1.1 meters tall. I was confused on the units at first, and had to google what "gm" is. If you mean "gram", I think writing it out would help not confuse people.

It's a pretty simple thing with lots of underlying info, and I liked it, but the image just confused me upon initial read. Gonna have to novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: there we go, all converted. And I am working on a few story elements, but it may take a day to get them done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with most of the above. The personalities in play are a good start, but there needs to be more to the story - otherwise this is just a random item generator. Something to draw the reader further in, something unexpected or surprising, would be helpful.

Also, please change all those ounce measures to SI units.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll definitely keep that in mind. I'm working on a few extra pieces/parts for this one, mostly in the form of transcription logs and tests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess I should have explained that a bit better. I'll find a way to say it doesn't give every ounce of knowledge, and that it's a bit mischievous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, you have the rough pieces in place. However, they don't seem to be focused enough. If you can come up with a troll logic-ish way to justify how the less conventional spawned items can be considered as toys, I think that will be what the article needs for impact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So they're researching the origins of the item even though the bunny already explained them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I went ahead and hotfixed out a few of the tests. Should read more streamlined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At this point, I'm guessing it will stick around, and performing the cleanup to remove the extraneous testing will give it an even better chance at survival. There's a lot of good bits to this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yea the tests which simply outline the parameters of its operation, combined with the list of results weren't remotely interesting1, which cinched my downvote.

Footnotes
1. aside from the footnote, and the implications of that item to begin with - good stuff there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much! I'll probably ad more relevant info if this stays up long enough to warrant it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I'll keep that in mind when I do a clean up round if it sticks around on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've got all the pieces. The teddy bear in the first test and the machete in the second test are good intimations regarding the way the anomaly works, while maintaining wonder. The personalities of the children add to the experience and that's well done.

However, I feel that a lot of the extra tests just pad it out and don't do anything appreciable to add to the story. You could probably summarize a whole bunch of the inactive tests and just say that yes, they found the limits to the test. Keeping the tests that showcase the intriguing aspects of how the anomaly functions are the best.

I'll +1, but there's room for cleanup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's pretty simplistic as far as these things go; maybe there could be more to the tests, but I feel you've got enough here to make me like the concept and properties at least. +1 from me.

item.title: A thank you and an image attribution.
item.content:encoded:
Hey everyone. This is my first scp, but I have writing experience in the past. Let me know what you all think! Regardless of if it gets taken down I'm still proud of it. Have a great day!

The photo comes from a local seller website where I asked the item and image owner if I could use it. the original image I can no longer find, sadly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yea I've been trying to fix it for the past 10 minutes or so, unsuccessfully

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OP, your image is broken.
